Where Did the Money Flow?

Your search for Women found 84 result(s)

Limited by country

CALIFORNIA LATINO WOMENS BOOK PROJECT (1)

United States / $5,000

Funds to assist in the production of a black-and-white photography book by Victoria G. Alvarado, which highlights California Latinas who are successfully impacting the well-being of the California Latino community.

Cultural Preservation, Inter-Cultural, Women

BATTERED WOMENS PROGRAM/YWCA OF SAN DIEGO (2)

United States / $5,000

Funds to assist in developing a Latina Battered Womens Community Outreach Program, including collateral bilingual materials

Health, Inter-Cultural, Women

JADE KING PRODUCTIONS: MULTI-ETHNIC RESOURCE WEB SITE (3)

United States / $4,000

Funds to help develop a screenwriters resource web site. It will focus on authentic multi-ethnic screen and television characters to challenge stereotypes and explore alternative options for expanding the range of ethnic characters in films.

Inter-Cultural, Media, Women

WOMENS BUSH MEDICINE (10)

Australia / $1,075

Funds to support a program that transmits traditional medicine from older women to young girls in a remote aboriginal Australian setting.

Children/Teens, Cultural Preservation, Education, Women

WOMENS CONFERENCE ON CONSCIOUS EATING (24)

United States / $400

Funds to support the conference on conscious eating as an act of healing.

Health, Spirituality, Women

MODEL MUGGING (30)

United States / $850

Funds to support a class for young teen women that addresses molestation/rape; setting boundaries, awareness and avoidance of danger; and practical life-saving fighting. Twenty-five girls were sponsored.

Children/Teens, Health, Women

MAIKO WOMENS DRUM AND POETRY ENSEMBLE (32)

United States / $3,000

Funds to support a 16-week course for high-school students. This program fosters self-esteem through percussion and vocal expression.

Arts, Children/Teens, Women

MUSLIM WOMEN IN ACTION (36)

United States / $250

Funds to support the social services, including a shelter, for Muslim women and children in San Diego.

Children/Teens, Inter-Cultural, Muslims, Women

MAYAN WEAVERS: Jolom Mayaetik (56)

Mexico / $5,000

Jolom Mayaetik, meaning "Mayan Weavers", is a collective founded in 1996. There are 859 indigenous women members who are weavers and represent several different areas in San Cristobal de Ls Casa, Chiaps, Mexico. It is an advocacy co-op program which focuses on ensuring that the members are assisted in many areas of business, fair trade, marketing, women's rights and the development of financial means to help support their families in food, housing, medicine and education.

Arts, Cultural Preservation, Women

TIBETAN DANCE PROJECT (58)

United States / $1,000

Funds to support Tsering Wangmo to teach traditional dancing to Urgan Tibetan girls in Seattle, WA

Arts, Cultural Preservation, Refugees, Women

REDE DE COMBATE AO CANCER (63)

Brazil / $800

"Guimar Pinheiro Franco" A group of volunteer women who care for poor cancer patients in hospitals (mainly children and older people).

Children/Teens, Elders, Health, Poverty, Women

MOSTEIRO DO SALVADOR  (68)

Brazil / $115

Benedictine Sisters in need of certain household and personal hygiene articles.

Poverty, Spirituality, Women

Zhinocha gromada (82)

Kazakhstan / $328

Buying a photocopier provided help and support to the low income families.

Advocacy, Children/Teens, Poverty, Women

"Anna alakani" (84)

Kazakhstan / $890

A mini micro credit program to bring single mothers out of poverty by organizing new jobs and mutual help groups. 1. Organizing mutual help group for women to help each other during the hard times. Money does not stay with one woman, its constantly going to the next person 2. This experience could be applied to the different regions and segments of the population who are in need of this kind of support This project will help 30 single mothers. In the first stage 5 women will receive the funding to start their own small businesses such as: baking pirozhki, making salads, dinners, renting a counter in the grocery store and doing laundry. After two months these women will give the start-up money to the next five women and so on.

Economic Development, Poverty, Women

Together Were a Family-Semyonovka Village,School (85)

Kazakhstan / $2,000

Parents can leave their children for several hours at the daycare, which gives them opportunity to find jobs, work at the potato fields. Four women were able to find jobs and formed a collective. They will start growing potatoes in the spring and contribute towards maintenance and growth of the daycare.

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Poverty, Women

"Beloshveika" Fund Osvobozhdenie" Pavlodar City (86)

Kazakhstan / $700

Acquiring sewing skills, sewing materials and creating the potential for former drug addicts to find jobs after the rehabilitation period.

Economic Development, Rehabilitation, Women

Phazenda Support center for single mothers (90)

Kazakhstan / $1,100

Helping mothers with young children who are temporarily unemployed. Using land for farming to create work for single mothers and their adult children.

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Economic Development, Women

" Were against poverty" (96)

Kazakhstan / $227

Union of mothers with many children, for eliminating poverty in the village. Flow Fund Gift:2 sewing machines, sewing supplies

Children/Teens, Economic Development, Poverty, Women

"Auizbirshilik" (Together) Organization "Kurdastar" (97)

Kazakhstan / $170

Support for low income families and families with many children. Flow Fund Gift:: 3 goats and a kid A family gives the offspring to a different low income family

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Economic Development, Women

Help yourself and others-Learn yourself and help others (99)

Kazakhstan / $2,000

Funding for two sewing and one knitting machine enabled workshop for making clothes Teaching women sewing skills decreasing unemployment Young people keep their national traditions alive making national costumes

Children/Teens, Cultural Preservation, Economic Development, Women

Your children love and wait for you (104)

Kazakhstan / $2,000

Practical and psychological support for women who lost custody of their children. 20 such women formed a group to speak up about their problems and support each other in the desire to be in contact with their kids.

Advocacy, Children/Teens, Women

Mini bakery- Ilgunova Irina Semipalatinsk (108)

Kazakhstan / $1,200

Single mothers opened this bakery, which will provide their families with baked goods. Some of them will sell the goods and have their own business. This bakery provided with place to work and have some extra income for single mothers.

Children/Teens, Economic Development, Health, Women

Kazakh National cultural Center (110)

Kazakhstan / $300

Teaching young adults Kazakh art. Flow Fund Gift: Sewing machine. We are planning to buy several more in order to open sewing business. Elderly women will be teaching young girls from Womens school how to make folk art items from fabric.

Arts, Children/Teens, Cultural Preservation, Economic Development, Women

Federation of business women (114)

Kazakhstan / $609

Women were able to pay the Land Ownership fees in order to keep the land they were working on. The land now will become their property, which will ensure further employment of 10 women with large families.

Advocacy, Agriculture, Economic Development, Women

Anarkulova Elmira (118)

Kazakhstan / $2,100

Sewing equipment and materials for a workshop. Working space for this project will be provided by local businessmen for free. A disabled woman, who is raising 2 children alone, will teach 7 young women sewing skills and employ them afterwards to make clothing for sale.

Disability, Economic Development, Women

Help yourself (134)

Kazakhstan / $800

A single mother requested help in buying a piece of land, a used motorcycle, a chain-saw, and 2 goats. At present this family has 4 people  mother and three children. The elder son has unstable low income; younger son goes to 8th grade of school. Mother works in a laboratory, which pays her $70 a month. With the motorcycle and chain-saw the sons will be able to work and make money cutting firewood for villagers. They will plant fruits and vegetables on land and support family budget. Motorcycle is necessary for transporting vegetables and fruits to the market. The family will cut firewood at low prices for the single mothers of our association.

Children/Teens, Economic Development, Women

Little Fairies (156)

Kazakhstan / $200

Teaching orphan children to adapt and provide for themselves in real life situations. Flow Fund Gift: Sewing machine. Teaching children to sew their own clothes. These machines will ensure that these girls will have a skill to use outside the orphanage.

Children/Teens, Economic Development, Education, Women

Preparation for a folk festival (163)

Kazakhstan / $1,450

Center for single mothers Preparation for a folk festival and chickens for production.

Agriculture, Arts, Cultural Preservation, Women

women's sewing project (164)

Kazakhstan / $450

Fabric for sewing projects

Economic Development, Women

Wheat planting (173)

Kazakhstan / $188

Flow Fund gift: Plowing and planting the seeds. This mother is able to grow wheat on her land and use the income to buy food for her farm animals and have seed for next year. Her hope is to have enough money to give her children a good education.

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Education, Women

"Umelie ruki" (176)

Kazakhstan / $1,300

Flow Fund Gift: 6 Sewing machines, and materials to make desks and chairs for sewing machines, carpentry equipment. We met with 6 women from village Dikhan, who are very skilled tailors, but had no start up capital to buy sewing machines and open their own business. We found out that in this village there were no sewing machines at all. They came up with wonderful plan. Local school gave them a room to put all the equipment and during the school hours girls are learning sewing and boys are learning carpentry, they made all the desks and chairs themselves. In the evening, women are using machines for their business. They made New Years costumes for children and from September 2005 they will receive orders to make school uniforms. Children from poor families will receive the uniforms for free. Regional administration decided to help too and is giving a salary to the sewing teacher.

Economic Development, Education, Women

"Under the Roof of my House" (179)

Kazakhstan / $203

Registration fees for the apartment, gas and water meters, bathroom pipes. This family was being evicted from their own house. Roza is a single mother with three young children and her monthly income is $18. She had no money to buy meters for gas and water, so authorities were charging her fixed fee, which is six times the amount she wouldve paid if she had the meters. Unfortunately, in our country, people who need help the most are unable to get it. Roza could be getting better help from the government if the apartment was in her name, but she has no money to cover the gas and water debts and pay the registration fees. We decided to help her with that. Now she is eligible for government support.

Advocacy, Poverty, Women

Traditional Dance (200)

Brazil / $1,155

Funds were continued to a group of women dancers on an Island off the coast of Bahia, Brazil who preserve their Afro-Brazilian traditions through dance and movement. This years funding supported them in their ongoing work of bringing Afro-Brazilian traditions to other Islands and the mainland and the creation of the 1st annual Dia Nacional da Consciencia Negro (A National Day of Black Consciousness).

Arts, Cultural Preservation, Women

Preservation of cultural traditions for young girls (202)

Brazil / $1,155

Funds were given to support the womans dance program on an Island off the coast of Bahia, Brazil to be able to extend itself to include girls of different ages. The young girls are taught, through dance and movement and story telling, the Afro-Brazilian traditions so that they may become tradition bearers in the future.

Arts, Children/Teens, Cultural Preservation, Women

Dana Pauly - Youth Sistership Grant (219)

United States / $1,000

Youth Sistership gift to support the work of a young woman devoted to agro-ecology. Dana was a leader in creating "Grown in Nelson," a farm-based group in Nelson County, Virginia that strengthens local agriculture by increasing new markets for local farmers. To this end, she helped secure a grant from a USDA-funded project: "Bridging the Urban/Rural Divide: marketing Local Food in the mid-Atlantic." Her contributions to sane farming practices included learning how to make Biodynamic Preps in anticipation of the major US producers retirement, and helping a small CSA farmer (Screech Owl Farm) expand her operations to a full-time farm.

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Cultural Preservation, Education, Women

Kristen Rothballer - Sistership Grant (220)

United States / $300

Kristen Rothballer This Sistership grant helped Kristen travel to Texas to support and document Diane Wilsons hunger strike in protest of Dow/Union Carbides pollution of the gulf and the possibility that Union Carbide/Dow may be released from responsibility for the Bhopal disaster in 1984. Kristen, 29, is currently the youth and community outreach manager for Bioneers/CHI and had been engaged in education, environmental and social justice issues for the past several years. She is passionate about inspiring people to follow their own path of discovery to earth activism.

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Education, Women

Alternatives for Girls (222)

United States / $400

This grant was by far my most creative and gratifying one in the Sistership category. While preparing to be a presenter at a conference entitled Woman and Power, a still, small voice told me to have travelers checks in hand and demonstrate the power of generosity by asking for elder caregivers, artists, and girls age 18 and younger to come tell me their dreams. The group of seven girls above came as a collective, and I learned how their work with Alternatives for Girls was helping them to change their lives around. They talked about their hopes for friends who were still struggling with drugs and difficult relationships. They also talked a bit, some shyly, about contributions they hoped to make to the world. I gave them each $25-$50 to support their individual dreams. And, most excitingly, I gave them $100 to contribute to their community. Their assignment: to decide amongst themselves how to wisely distribute the funds and to let me know how they did it. They divvied the gift up equally to purchase lunch and toysfun and food.

Children/Teens, Education, Spirituality, Women

The People's Grocery (224)

United States / $1,000

This seed grant supports a budding cooperative a cooperative, youth run grocery store and entrepreneurial training institute in West Oakland. Their goal is to provide affordable organic food, jobs and job training, community economic development, urban gardens, and social and environmental justice education to the West Oakland community. They have a strong advisory and working board and have already established a small garden.

Agriculture, Children/Teens, Ecology/Sustainability, Economic Development, Women

The S.E.E.D. Project on Inclusive Curriculum (225)

United States / $2,000

Undesignated support for program that has created and sustains an international community of educators devoted to developing more inclusive curriculum K-12. Teams of educators attend a week-long training and return home to co-conduct ongoing study groups of teachers and administrators, complete with support materials. Key questions for all participants in SEED seminars are: What would curriculum and pedagogy look like if the diverse lives of women and girls were seen as co-central with the diverse lives of men and boys? And how can curriculum and teaching methods provide, in the metaphors of Emily Style, both windows into others experiences, and mirrors of each students own reality and validity.

Children/Teens, Education, Women

Kembatta Womens Self-Help Center (KMG) (226)

Ethiopia / $3,000

Bogaletch Gebre embodies Ghandis call for us to be the change we want to see in the world. She was the first girl in her village/district to be educated beyond the fourth grade. After studying microbiology, physiology, epidemiology and public health in Israel and the US, she taught health sciences in Ethiopia and the US before returning home to establish KMG. While lead by women, KMG works wholistically, via three interrelated programs that help women and their families to help themselves improve community: - Health: Reproductive health education, including the elimination of female genital excision (FGE) and HIV/AIDS prevention - Livelihood: Vocational training and advancement of womens entrepreneurial skills - Environment: Restoring depleted watersheds and damaged ecosystems.

Advocacy, Children/Teens, Ecology/Sustainability, Economic Development, Women

Maher Center women's refuge (227)

India / $2,500

Sister Lucy, with the support of Father DSas founded the Maher Center as first refuge for battered women in the city of Pune in 1997. Since then, this remarkably caste-free, interfaith, ecologically operated center has become instrumental in opening satellite centers: caste-free zones of protection and community transformation.

Ecology/Sustainability, Health, Inter-Cultural, Women

Nganga and Village Health Project (229)

Zimbabwe / $2,000

The Nganga Project, a non-profit organization, recognizes that collaboration with traditional healers is essential in confronting AIDS in Africa, and so is working in alliance with the native ways of traditional African people to address AIDS, hunger and other community issues. The project seeks to establish alliances between practitioners of different healing modalities  traditional, Western, Chinese, Native American, etc. Starvation continues to be a major cause of death in Zimbabwe. In addition, Zimbabwe suffers from the highest infection rate of AIDS in the world: 25% of the population is HIV positive. Traditional rites of initiation for young men and women have been largely lost in the past two generations, which the elders see as directly connected to widespread alienation among youth and the exacerbation of the local AIDS crisis. The Nganga Project is collaborating to revive the old rites and make them relevant for those coming of age in contemporary Zimbabwe. The Village Health Project seeks to help alleviate the suffering of those with HIV/AIDS in Zimbabwe by starting a free Chinese Medicine clinic there.

Cultural Preservation, Health, Spirituality, Women

Parbhani Seed Savers, (236)

India / $496

The project promotes growing of native vegetable varieties in home gardens by women in efforts to improve the nutritional security of women and children. Apart from conservation of traditional vegetable varieties, the project helps revive the tradition of backyard home gardens and also make a significant contribution to the food and nutritional security of poor families.

Agriculture, Biodiversity, Cultural Preservation, Women

Seed training for staff of the Planting Material Network (240)

Solomon Islands / $709

Planting Material Network long-time staff member, Nancy Oelo, has been chosen to attend seed saving training in Australia. Nancy has worked for the PMN for five years in various capacities and is very hard working. She will attend an International Development Course, a Local Seed Network course and the Seed Savers' Eighteenth Annual Conference in October 2005 in Australia. Nancy will be in a group with participants from GREEN Foundation, India (supported by Flow Fund) and Papua New Guinea as well as from Australia. Flow Fund is paying for her fares. Seed Savers is offering the training and accommodation.

Agriculture, Biodiversity, Ecology/Sustainability, Education, Women

Self-development programme for activist (248)

Myanmar / $1,000

This scholarship is for capacity building for an outstanding activist in Burma. She has been working as programme director of Metta Development Foundation, since its conception more than 5 years ago. Metta is the first real local NGO in Burma, and it concentrates on empowering grassroots community development work for self-reliance. This organisation provides people at the grassroots level throughout Burma with valuable and effective programmes to empower communities and individuals and improve the capacity for community development. For example Womens Development, Early Childhood Care & Development, Community-managed Healthcare, Rural Reconstruction, Farmer Field School for Sustainable Agriculture, etc. With support from the Flow Fund, she plans to visit an alternative community in the UK in order to enhance her knowledge about alternative development. She will explore the ideas of alternative community management and other issues so that she can apply this appropriately to her work in Burma. She will also have the opportunity to visit some international NGOs.

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Health, Inter-Cultural, Women

Consortium for Young Women (258)

United States / $4,000

This organization works with young girls to provide them with different ways of relating to the world and different way of better taking care of themselves.

Children/Teens, Education, Spirituality, Women

Kenya Jordana James (260)

United States / $500

Kenya Jordana James has been a innovator almost from the time of her birth in 1989. Growing up in New York City with a mom and Great Grandfather that were deeply involved in their community, Kenya's entire life has been defined and enhanced by the village that has raised her. In many ways, she is a child of the movement. Tired of reading magazines that didn't speak directly to her peers, Kenya decided to create her own. Launched with the savings from her baking business, Kenyajordana cakes, where she baked cakes from scratch and from a previous jewelry-making venture, Kenya launched Blackgirl Magazine. She currently hosts Sessions, a lively discussion between artists, and the teen community. Usually held in a intimate setting this event provides the opportunity for artists and community to come together to exchange ideas and information.

Arts, Children/Teens, Media, Women

Choreography, women and ecology - Cultural Center Chandralekha (287)

India / $2,500

Chandralekha, India's most controversial choreographer broke new ground in the 1980s when she merged traditional Easter and modern Western forms.

Arts, Cultural Preservation, Inter-Cultural, Women

Whistle Home and Oasis Project for mothers and children with HIV (294)

Thailand / $2,500

Last year Whistle Home, a home that provides child care for mothers with HIV, almost had to terminate the program because of lack of funding, but the flow fund made it possible to continue. This year the grant continues to provide child care for mothers with HIV. In the morning, they can leave their children at Whistle Home and go to work. Children receive food, diapers, and nourishment. In the evening the mothers come to take their children back home. Since mothers with HIV cannot breast feed their children, the Home also provides instant milk powder for free. Some children have been left at Whistle Home because their mothers died. So the home provides shelters and food for them too. Chansuda Suwanchandee was a woman with HIV who started and ran the Oasis Project. It was her dream to provide shelter, food, and health care for homeless people with HIV in Bangkok. What she had already done in the first phase of this project was getting to know homeless people with HIV and their situation. She lived and ate with them on the streets. When some of them needed medication, she helped them in whatever ways she could. Many of them did not have any legal documents. Chansuda helped them find these documents so that they could use social welfare for their medication. As a single mother of three young kids and a person with HIV, Chansuda was an example of a person who never gave up and always contributed in whatever ways she could to the well-beings of others.

Children/Teens, Disability, Health, Poverty, Women

Womens Studies - Sangha Savika (295)

Thailand / $2,500

Buddhism came to Thailand 2000 years ago and it is the major religion. However, women are considered inferior and womens ordination is even illegal. Women who seek ordination have to go to Sri Lanka to be ordained, but they are not totally accepted by the Buddhist institute. Some of the women sought ordination from Thai male monks secretly and cannot live openly. Sangha Savika is a group of lay women who realize such problems. So they aim to support spiritual practice of Buddhist women, womens ordination, and the community of female monks. Sangha Savika has used half of the money to help build a place for spiritual practice for women. Women and female monks can come to practice here. Female monks who have no place to stay can also live here. The other half of the money has been used to run four seminars and retreats which aim to support womens ordination, visit communities of female monks around the country, and help individual female monks, who still have no place to stay, find a community to live and practice with.

Spirituality, Women

Radio International Feminista (308)

Costa Rica / $1,000

Based in Colon, Costa Rica, has done path breaking work on organizing women for peace and environmental work. This funding will allow them to help women in Central American countries on dialogue and action for disarmament and demilitarization.

Advocacy, Education, Media, Peace, Women

Tibetan Buddhist Nuns Shoto Terdrom (319)

China / $1,000

Tibetan Buddhist Nuns Shoto Terdrom Dligum Valley I fell in love with these nuns who live in a cave hennitage at 16,000 feet in one of Tibet's most sacred sites. It is the area where carved "Rainbow Bodhisattva" in one of their local caves. They are extremely poor but courageous and joyous women. A great inspiration.

Cultural Preservation, Spirituality, Women

Siberian Womens Circle (320)

Russian Federation / $2,000

Siberian Womens Circle At the time of the World Wheel in Siberia, a women's group was formed out of the inspiration of our time together. These women meet regularly and not only support each other but address the needs of their environment at this very difficult time for Russia.

Ecology/Sustainability, Women

Sri Sarada Society (324)

United States / $1,000

Sri Sarada Society Women are given a platform to teach and express their spiritual insights.

Spirituality, Women

Cottage Industry for Village Women (329)

India / $2,000

Training women to weave, make cotton cloth etc. and to start small businesses.

Ecology/Sustainability, Economic Development, Women

Bosnian Women (331)

Bosnia and Herzegovina / $1,000

Edie Harshome met with grass roots women's groups in Dubrovnik Sarajevo trying to help refugee women reorganize their lives and regain a means of lively hood after the war. This includes development of small handicrafts that Edie brought back to US to sell and raise money for the Women's Center, Desa in Sarajevo.

Advocacy, Economic Development, Women

Perzent Karalkalpakistan, Uzbekistan (334)

Uzbekistan / $2,500

Many large international agencies have done research in the Aral Sea area, but few actually have operational programs. Mothers' breast milk is toxic, the drinking water is polluted, and there is the highest rate of infant mortality in the FSU. I first funded a pamphlet written in the Karalkalpak language for teenagers who marry and have children at 16-19 years of age, concerning sanitation, drinking water, and health. After consulting with Oral Atanyazova in Nukus, I've decided to additionally fund a monthly women's bulletin based loosely on the book "Our Bodies, Ourselves" Oral is an independent woman Gynecologist and now Ph.d, of Karalkalpak origin, who is deeply committed to the health of her people and is bursting with good ideas. She is a real hope for change and I am working to get UNICEF involved with her visionary future for the women of her region.

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Health, Nuclear, Water, Women

Beijing Conference fees (335)

Russian Federation, Uzbekistan / $600

Beijing Conference fees I am trying to raise the money for both of the women heading two projects, to represent areas of extreme ecological devastation at the Beijing Women's Conference in September. One participant has already raised her own airfare.

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Health, Water, Women

Pamphlet on Safe Energy Berkeley, CA (343)

United States / $2,000

Pamphlet on Safe Energy I have been disturbed at the lack of information worldwide about the dangers of nuclear energy for peaceful uses, written in a form accessible to people who are not already "on board". I wanted to emphasize the health risks and available energy alternatives written from a culturally sensitive viewpoint for Russians, Chinese, Japanese. A group of Berkeley women called Council for Safe Energy are writing a fourteen page simple illustrated appealing booklet geared to concern for children with the purpose of getting women aboard, and giving them the information they need to convince others. The target date for publication is in August 1995 for the Beijing Women's Forum and for the Alternative Energy Workshop in Moscow in November.

Advocacy, Children/Teens, Ecology/Sustainability, Nuclear, Women

Unlimited Possibilities Award to Ludmilla Zhirina (353)

Russian Federation / $645

There are some people who you know are working every day to make a difference and will use whatever money you give them thoughtfully with the idea of making an impact. Ludmilla is one of those people. She comes from a small city near the Ukrainian border that was the most heavily radiated as a result of the Chernobyl accident. She was inspired by our alternative energy seminars and the information she heard at the 10th Anniversary of Chernobyl conference. When I offered her the grant of $500 with no strings attached, she immediately produced a simple booklet on energy production in her area to present at a series of seminars which she did for teachers who are working in schools and environmental centers throughout her region. She reports that her booklet is very popular and that there is a demand for expanding the energy seminars. Booklets will be used by the teachers with their students throughout her area and she wants further help in expanding the energy seminars. Previously, I provided her with a $145 grant for supplies and translation for an experiential ecology seminar

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Education, Media, Nuclear, Women

Clean and Healthy Food Production Project (354)

Russian Federation / $500

Dana -a Nikolaev women's organization Unlimited Possibilities Award to Natasha Pokrass a 'Can-Do' lady. When we did a check-in at the women's circle to see what the life focus was for each participant. Most said "family" but not Natasha. Although she has a family, her goal was to help make positive changes in the health of her community.. "She's not happy unless she's too busy" said a friend at the circle. She has already found a donation of bread baking machines and is sponsoring a seminar to encourage and support others to produce healthy clean (as opposed to irradiated) food.

Advocacy, Ecology/Sustainability, Health, Women

Lokenath Divine Life Mission (378)

India / $500

Lokenath Divine Life Mission Mission projects include the establishment of women's empowerment groups, women's healthcare, child education, creative development, adult education, eye surgery & care, homeopathy, farmers clubs/conservation awareness. The Mission is committed to raising the socio-economic level of the impoverished people of the slums and villages in and around Calcutta, and addresses fundamental problems at the grass root level. They conduct school classes "on the streets" and health care in a medical mobile where care is needed most.

Health, Poverty, Spirituality, Women

Education of Cambodian Women. (388)

Cambodia / $2,500

Sokhoeun fled from Cambodia as a young woman and came to the U.S. She and her husband own the Cambodian restaurant Ankor Wat. They have supported many hundreds of Cambodian refugees, and scores of artists and cultural figures in maintaining their heritage. She studied International Studies and Buddhism at C.I.I.S and traveled frequently to Cambodia to help women in business and grassroots organizing. Encouraged by many Buddhist teachers, including Maha Goshnanda, she developed this activity into the Naga Women's Party. The grant money was spent for the education of Cambodian women.

Education, Women

Increasing the financial self-sufficiency of Maohi women. (396)

French Polynesia / $1,000

HITI TAU, a network of locally based community organizations throughout the islands of French-Occupied Polynesia, is also the host of Tahiti's biggest antinuclear organization. Hiti Tau is searching for ways to become politically, culturally and financially independent from nuclear colonialism. Mrs. Vaihere Bordes, an energetic Maohi woman, organized the women's cooperative HOTU TAIRE MAOHI to produce traditional coconut oil also known as "monoi". This grant will be used to find ways to distribute the products and increase the financial self-sufficiency of Maohi women.

Ecology/Sustainability, Economic Development, Nuclear, Women

Radio series on Women for Plutonium Free World (406)

United States / $500

Radio producer Penny Rosenwasser and writer/activist Margaret Pavel produced a series of four one-hour radio programs on KPFA Brainstorm, interviewing Priscilla Settee, Esther Yazzie (Navaho leader), Mildred McClain, Claire Greensfelder, Fusako de Angelis. We also made tapes to distribute among the women's communities and more for radio broadcasting

Advocacy, Media, Nuclear, Women

Legal Literacy in India (407)

India / $10,000

The Justice Sunanda Bhandare Foundation was established in 1994 to facilitate a more gender-equitable society in India. In the course of its work, it has embraced women as their main constituents, in addition to the physically challenged and the vast majority of Indian people who live in villages. In India, 49%) of women and 30% of the total population is illiterate. While the National Literacy Mission has embarked on a massive literacy project throughout the country, this Foundation is concerned with spreading awareness of the legal rights, particularly those of women. Its legal literacy mission is designed to make more people aware of their legal rights as well as gender biases in laws, so that eventually public opinion will support the removal of these anomalies. The courts in India are becoming more pro-active, particularly when addressing women's rights in the areas of maintenance, divorce, inheritance and adoption. The' Foundation's experience shows, however, that very often the women themselves seem unaware of these rights.

Advocacy, Education, Women

Nun Ordination (415)

Thailand, Sri Lanka / $350

This grant helped support the travel of three Theravaden nuns from Thailand to Sri Lanka to study and take full bikkhuni ordination. There has been no full ordination of nuns in Thailand for 1200 years. As a result, nuns have a surprisingly low status in this patriarchal culture. There is a growing movement among women activists to improve the role of nuns and we are actively involved in this work.

Spirituality, Women

Pema lJn Mingaborn, Tibetan Nun - visit to USA (421)

India, United States / $1,200

Pema lJn Mingaborn, Tibetan Nun, Dharamsala India This grant award is to pay the airfare of Perna Mingabom to come to Boulder, Colorado for a period of study at Naropa University. Naropa has agreed to offer a full scholarship for her. In return we said we would pay her traveling costs. She is the head of a monastery of nuns in Dharmasala, India, and wishes to further her dharma education to bring back the teachings to her sister nuns. Perna speaks and writes English.

Education, Spirituality, Women

Ekta Parishad, organization for indigenous people's rights, Madya Pradesh, (431)

India / $1,200

During the Solidarity Walks we had many discussions with the representative from the indigenous people's movement in India, Ekta Parishad (this name means "Forum of Unity"). He spoke of the inspiring work they are doing regarding advancing women's rights and power within traditional society, as well as their work protecting tribal·land rights and tenure in the face of development pressures.

Cultural Preservation, Ecology/Sustainability, Women

Rural community Development Grants (440)

Lao People's Democratic Republic, India / $2,000

One grant enabled women in the region to bring in a traditional weaving expert from Vientiene to show them saleable patterns and help develop a distribution plan B. K. Another grant enabled a group of tribal artisans to produce a marketing brochure to advertise their beautiful designs of locally handmade clay products for distribution to interior designers and architects in southern India. Jinan's work in developing this cooperative literally saved the economy of a destitute village in Kerala. This project needs more financial help but we consider it a realistic model of both alternative arts and crafts and appropriate-scale economic development.

Arts, Ecology/Sustainability, Economic Development, Women

Travel grant for community leader (442)

Myanmar / $200

A leading Kachin woman for travel through northern Burma to meet with Baptist women's groups about alternative development projects.

Cultural Preservation, Ecology/Sustainability, Women

Women's Environment and Development Organization (446)

United States / $500

This was a small general support grant to a (big) sister organization whose work we respect highly.

Ecology/Sustainability, Education, Women

Tibetan Nuns: Shugsep Education Project Funds (459)

India / $5,000

Gift to assist the educational program, including teachers' salaries and educational materials at Shugsep, a nunnery that supports Tibetan Buddhist nuns of the Nyingma sect. Dharamsala, India

Education, Women

European Tibetan Nuns Project (475)

India / $1,500

A travel scholarship for a project based in Europe that supports Tibetan Buddhist nuns.

Cultural Preservation, Spirituality, Women

Tibetan Womens Association (487)

India / $10,000

Tibetan Womens Association Scholarship to cover board, logding and tuition for one Tibetan refugee nuns to pursue Buddhist Philosophy study for 11 years at Dolmaling Nunnery and for 2 refugee nuns to study for 9 years at Shungseb Nunnery.

Cultural Preservation, Spirituality, Women

Refugee children and adolescent girls in Darfur (496)

Sudan / $2,000

Refugee children and adolescent girls in Darfur Almost 2 million people in the three states of Darfur are made refugee or internally displaced, due to the violent conflict that started in March 2003. More than 60% of them are children under 18 years of age. In the state of South Darfur alone, hundreds of thousands of people are seeking refuge in camps, lacking basic services and security. In order to provide the children with structure and a sense of normality, SPCR established nine Children Centers in 3 locations in South Darfur where internally displaced people live. 40 Local animators are trained in organizing every day social and recreational activities for almost 10.000 children from different ages. The nine centers are also serving the community and function as informal town halls where the community gathers to discuss issues of concern, receive vaccinations against cholera, measles and typhus and learn about general issues like hygiene and protection against violence. One of the activities is aimed at adolescent girls. Many of them experienced during the conflict sexual abuse, sometimes resulting in an unwanted pregnancy. SPCR offers them in the afternoon the opportunity to meet in the center and make handicrafts while talking to each other and the animators about their experiences. At present more than 500 girls meet and encourage each other, to cope and accept their often unwanted baby. As the needs are enormous, the contribution of the Flow Fund of $1,500 for the children activities and $500 for more the adolescent girls project, were greatly appreciated.

Children/Teens, Health, Poverty, Refugees, Women

Archive of Feminist Peace Education (502)

Germany / $0

Ellen Diederich is a well known feminist peace activist. She has built up an archive over several decades. She was contemplating tclosing down the archive for want of funds. But now she has an offer to move to an ecovillage where she can develop new forms of peace work. "Flow fund" money is helping her to develop materials for school children to teach them about how to make peace.

Children/Teens, Education, Peace, Women

Center for Protection of Women and Children (504)

Serbia and Montenegro / $2,000

Center for Protection of Women and Children Kosovo

Children/Teens, Health, Peace, Women

Traditional knowledge in Central America (506)

/ $500

The Flow Fund gift was to a woman working with womens healing using traditional herbs and medicines.

Cultural Preservation, Health, Women

Womens Clinic (507)

Nicaragua / $350

Womens Clinic Teaching women economic skills

Economic Development, Health, Women

Women for Water (511)

United States / $1,000

Water, Women

Three Women from Flower Aceh (513)

Indonesia / $2,500

Three Women from Flower Aceh The Flow Fund Gift paid for 3 women from Aceh, Indonesia to attend the Sustainability Leadership Training program.  This training was for activists, community leaders, and representatives from organizations primarily around Asia at Pun Pun, an earth-house organic community based in Chiengmai, Thailand.   Of all the areas hit by the tsunami, the Indonesian province of Aceh, suffered most. That happened to an Aceh where a bloody conflict has been raging for 28 years between the Indonesian army and the Free Aceh Movement (GAM). For 15 years now, the womens organisation Flower Aceh has been actively helping women who have fallen victim to violence, including torture and rape. Staff members are regularly threatened and hampered in their job.   And now, after the tsunami, surrounded by international relief, they also work together with other human rights and womens organisations for the victims of the flood. In the aftermath of the tsunami, Flower Aceh is assessing women's needs, providing basic necessities and running a Women's Crisis Centre in conjunction with other local women's groups. Prior to the disaster, Flower Aceh was the first women's NGO in Aceh to address human rights abuses against women, such as mass rape. Using the experience garnered from empowering women in conflict situations, Flower Aceh is working to include women in the reconstruction process in one of the regions most severely devastated by the earthquake and tsunami.   The staff of Flower Aceh, one of the oldest womens rights organizations in the province, escaped the tsunami by running to the second floor of a building nearby. From there, they watched as their office was swept away below them.   Since 1989, Flower Aceh has researched the impact of cultural practices and the ongoing conflict on women in Aceh. This information  unique and irreplaceable  was gone in minutes. The organizations garden, containing more than 600 plants used for traditional medicine, was also destroyed

Advocacy, Cultural Preservation, Peace, Women

Ladakh Nunnery Center Building Project (515)

India / $1,250

Tsering Palm is a Tibetan Buddhist nun living in Ladakh, India. She is also a Tibetan traditional medicine doctor and a director of a nunnery school which support young women from poor families to gain proper education. There are twenty nuns staying at the nunnery center. Five of them are staff and rest are students. There is big shortage of room. In each room six nuns are staying together. Seven nuns are staying in the office. The Flow Fund gift supported new rooms to be built for the nuns

Cultural Preservation, Refugees, Spirituality, Women

Art for new Planned Parenthood Clinic (519)

United States / $6,000

The Flow Funder and five artists met with architects, designers and management to discuss the use of art throughout this new womens health facility which serves women from all socio-economic backgrounds. The goal was to provide high quality fine art which would create a soothing and healing atmosphere. The Flow Fund gifts were given to the five artists who created and donated the art.

Arts, Health, Women
